questions.to_find: The output printed by the program when unknown is called with a[1] and a[2] as var (reference) parameters.

understand.plain: The procedure 'unknown' takes two var (call-by-reference) parameters b and c. Inside, it first sets a[i]=i making the array [1,2,3,4,5], then sets b:=0 and c:=0. Since b aliases a[1] and c aliases a[2], after these assignments a becomes [0,0,3,4,5]. The procedure then prints all five elements.

keywords.explain: The formal parameter is an alias for the actual argument variable; any change to the formal parameter directly changes the actual argument.
keywords.example: If b is a var param aliasing a[1], then b:=0 sets a[1] to 0.

keywords.explain: Two names refer to the same memory location. Here b and a[1] are aliases, as are c and a[2].

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='520' height='180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='500' height='160' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='35' class='lbl ink'>Array a after for-loop: a = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]</text><rect x='20' y='50' width='80' height='40' class='hi'/><rect x='100' y='50' width='80' height='40' class='hi'/><rect x='180' y='50' width='80' height='40' class='bg'/><rect x='260' y='50' width='80' height='40' class='bg'/><rect x='340' y='50' width='80' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='60' y='76' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>1</text><text x='140' y='76' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>2</text><text x='220' y='76' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>3</text><text x='300' y='76' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>4</text><text x='380' y='76' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>5</text><text x='60' y='108' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>a[1]=b</text><text x='140' y='108' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>a[2]=c</text><text x='20' y='135' class='lbl ink'>After b:=0; c:=0 => a = [</text><text x='230' y='135' class='lbl no'>0</text><text x='250' y='135' class='lbl ink'>, </text><text x='265' y='135' class='lbl no'>0</text><text x='285' y='135' class='lbl ink'>, 3, 4, 5]  Output: 0 0 3 4 5</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Array a is first filled as [1,2,3,4,5]. Since b aliases a[1] and c aliases a[2], setting b:=0 and c:=0 changes the array to [0,0,3,4,5]. The write loop then prints 0 0 3 4 5.

given.aim: Trace the program output when unknown is called with a[1] and a[2] as var (reference) parameters.

terms.meaning: b is a call-by-reference parameter; it is an alias for the actual argument a[1].
terms.connects: Any assignment to b directly modifies a[1].

terms.meaning: c is a call-by-reference parameter aliasing a[2].
terms.connects: Any assignment to c directly modifies a[2].

terms.meaning: Initializes the array so a[1]=1, a[2]=2, ..., a[5]=5.
terms.connects: Sets up the array values before the b and c assignments.

given.plan: Step 1: The procedure first sets a[i]=i (i=1..5), giving array [1,2,3,4,5]. Step 2: b:=0 sets a[1]=0 (aliasing); c:=0 sets a[2]=0 (aliasing). Array becomes [0,0,3,4,5]. Step 3: The write loop prints all five: 0 0 3 4 5.

steps.title: Initialize array inside procedure
steps.apply: After the first for loop: a = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]. At this point b aliases a[1]=1 and c aliases a[2]=2.
steps.note: The array is filled inside the procedure, not before the call.

steps.title: Apply b:=0 and c:=0 (call-by-reference effect)
steps.apply: b:=0 modifies a[1] to 0 (b is an alias for a[1]). c:=0 modifies a[2] to 0. Array is now [0, 0, 3, 4, 5].
steps.note: This is the key effect of call-by-reference: assignments through b/c change the original array.

steps.title: Print the array
steps.note: The local i in the procedure shadows the global i, so the loop uses the local i from 1 to 5.

questions.formula_note: Call-by-reference parameter passing: formal parameter is an alias for the actual argument; assignment to formal immediately updates the actual.

questions.question:
Indicate all the true statements from the following:

A. Recursive descent parsing cannot be used for grammar with left recursion.
B. The intermediate form for representing expressions which is best suited for code optimization is the postfix form.
C. A programming language not supporting either recursion or pointer type does not need the support of dynamic memory allocation.
D. Although C does not support call-by-name parameter passing, the effect can be correctly simulated in C.
E. No feature of Pascal typing violates strong typing in Pascal.
questions.to_find: All true statements from the given list about compiler design concepts.

understand.plain: This question tests knowledge of several compiler design topics: parsing techniques, intermediate code forms, memory allocation, parameter passing, and type systems. We must identify which statements are factually correct.

keywords.explain: A top-down parsing technique where each non-terminal has a corresponding procedure. It fails on left-recursive grammars because it enters infinite recursion.
keywords.example: Grammar A -> Ab | c is left-recursive; the procedure for A immediately calls itself, looping forever.

keywords.explain: A parameter passing mechanism where the actual argument expression is re-evaluated every time the formal parameter is used (like a macro substitution). It cannot be fully simulated in C because C lacks the ability to pass unevaluated expressions.
keywords.example: swap(a[i], i) behaves differently under call-by-name vs call-by-reference.

keywords.explain: Runtime heap allocation (malloc/new). Required when sizes or lifetimes cannot be determined at compile time, typically needed for recursion (stack frames) and pointers.
keywords.example: Recursive function calls need dynamic stack allocation. Without recursion or pointers, stack frames are fixed and static allocation suffices.

understand.visual_alt: Analysis of four compiler-related statements: A (true - left recursion breaks recursive descent), B (false - three-address code is better than postfix for optimization), C (true - no recursion/pointers means static allocation suffices), D (false - call-by-name cannot be fully simulated in C), E (false - Pascal variant records violate strong typing).

given.aim: Determine which of the five statements about compiler concepts are true.

terms.meaning: A production of the form A -> A alpha; causes recursive descent parsers to loop infinitely.
terms.connects: Statement A says recursive descent cannot handle this — which is TRUE.

terms.meaning: Postfix is good for evaluation but three-address code (TAC) is the standard intermediate representation for code optimization because it exposes data flow and control flow clearly.
terms.example: TAC: t1=a+b; t2=t1*c allows easy CSE and data-flow analysis.
terms.connects: Statement B claims postfix is best — this is FALSE.

terms.meaning: If a language has no recursion and no pointers, all data sizes and lifetimes are known at compile time, so static allocation is sufficient.
terms.example: FORTRAN 66 had no recursion; compilers could statically allocate all activation records.
terms.connects: Statement C claims no dynamic allocation needed — this is TRUE.

given.plan: Evaluate each statement against standard compiler design theory. A is true (left recursion breaks recursive descent). B is false (postfix is not the best for optimization; DAG/TAC is). C is true (no recursion + no pointers => no dynamic memory needed). D is false (call-by-name requires passing unevaluated expression thunks, which C cannot fully support with standard constructs). E is false (Pascal variant records are a well-known violation of strong typing).

steps.title: Evaluate statements A and B
steps.apply:
A: TRUE. Recursive descent parsing for a grammar with production A -> A alpha causes the procedure for A to immediately call itself infinitely. Left recursion must be eliminated first.
B: FALSE. While postfix notation is useful for stack-based evaluation, three-address code (TAC) or DAGs are the intermediate forms best suited for code optimization because they support data-flow analysis, common subexpression elimination, etc.
steps.note: Postfix form is compact but lacks explicit temporaries that optimization algorithms need.

steps.title: Evaluate statements C and D
steps.apply:
C: TRUE. Dynamic memory allocation (heap) is required for recursion (variable-depth call stacks) and pointer-based data structures. If neither is supported, all activation records and arrays can be statically allocated at compile time.
D: FALSE. Call-by-name requires that the actual argument expression be re-evaluated each time the formal parameter is accessed (pass a thunk). In C, you can approximate this with function pointers or macros, but it is not a correct simulation in general (e.g., side-effects in the argument expression may not behave identically).
steps.note: Statement D is debatable in some textbooks, but standard GATE answer treats it as false.

steps.title: Evaluate statement E
steps.apply: E: FALSE. Pascal's variant records (unions discriminated by a tag field) allow the tag to be changed independently of the actual stored value, enabling type-unsafe accesses. This is a well-known violation of strong typing in Pascal.
steps.note: Strong typing means every value has a well-defined type and no implicit conversions bypass type checks; Pascal's variant records break this.

solution.result: True statements: A and C. Answer: A, C.

questions.formula_note: Left-recursion elimination is required before applying recursive descent parsing. Static allocation suffices when neither recursion nor pointer types are present.

questions.to_find: The values of x and z printed after calling P(x, x*y, z) under each of three parameter-passing mechanisms.

understand.plain: Procedure P takes three parameters a, b, c. It sets a:=2 then c:=a+b. We call P(x, x*y, z) with x=1, y=5, z=100. The output depends on whether parameters are passed by value (copies made), by reference (aliases to originals), or by name (argument expressions re-evaluated on each use).

keywords.explain: Actual argument values are copied into formal parameters. Changes to formals do not affect actuals.
keywords.example: P(x, x*y, z) with call-by-value: a=1, b=5, c=100 (copies). Changing a inside P does not change x.

keywords.explain: Formal parameters are aliases (pointers) to actual arguments. Changes to formals directly change actuals. Note: expressions like x*y are passed as a temporary value, not an l-value.
keywords.example: a aliases x, so a:=2 sets x=2.

keywords.explain: Formal parameter is textually substituted by the actual argument expression, which is re-evaluated every time the formal is used (like a macro). Assignments to formals affect the actual l-value expression.
keywords.example: a is 'x', b is 'x*y' (re-evaluated on each access). After a:=2 (x=2), b re-evaluates as x*y = 2*5 = 10.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='560' height='220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='5' y='5' width='550' height='210' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='15' y='30' class='lbl ink'>P(x, x*y, z) with x=1, y=5, z=100</text><text x='15' y='56' class='tag mut'>Mechanism</text><text x='150' y='56' class='tag mut'>a:=2 effect</text><text x='310' y='56' class='tag mut'>b value</text><text x='420' y='56' class='tag mut'>c:=a+b => z</text><rect x='10' y='62' width='535' height='1' class='bg'/><text x='15' y='90' class='sub ink'>By Value</text><text x='150' y='90' class='sub ink'>a=2 (copy), x=1</text><text x='310' y='90' class='sub ink'>5 (copy)</text><text x='420' y='90' class='sub ink'>c=7 (copy), z=100</text><text x='15' y='120' class='sub ink'>By Reference</text><text x='150' y='120' class='sub ok'>x=2</text><text x='310' y='120' class='sub ink'>5 (temp)</text><text x='420' y='120' class='sub ok'>z=2+5=7</text><text x='15' y='150' class='sub ink'>By Name</text><text x='150' y='150' class='sub ok'>x=2</text><text x='310' y='150' class='sub no'>x*y=2*5=10</text><text x='420' y='150' class='sub no'>z=2+10=12</text><text x='15' y='185' class='lbl ink'>Output: Value: x=1,z=100 | Ref: x=2,z=7 | Name: x=2,z=12</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Table comparing three parameter passing mechanisms for P(x, x*y, z): by-value produces x=1,z=100 (no changes visible outside); by-reference produces x=2,z=7 (a:=2 modifies x, c:=a+b=7 modifies z); by-name produces x=2,z=12 (b re-evaluates as x*y=2*5=10 after x was changed to 2).

given.aim: Compute the output x= and z= after P(x, x*y, z) under pass-by-value, pass-by-reference, and pass-by-name.

terms.meaning: Starting state before the procedure call.
terms.example: x*y evaluates to 1*5=5 at call time.
terms.connects: Used as the actual arguments in the call P(x, x*y, z).

terms.meaning: Two statements: first set a to 2, then set c to the sum a+b.
terms.example: If a aliases x and c aliases z: x becomes 2, z becomes 2+b.
terms.connects: The order matters: a is changed first, then c uses the new a.

terms.meaning: Under call-by-name, x*y is an expression substituted for b; it is re-evaluated when b is read. Under reference, x*y is evaluated once at call time to a temporary.
terms.example: By-name: when c:=a+b is reached and x has already become 2, b re-evaluates as 2*5=10.
terms.connects: This is why by-name produces z=12 (2+10) rather than z=7 (2+5).

given.plan: Simulate each parameter passing mechanism: (i) by-value: make copies, changes don't propagate back; (ii) by-reference: bind a->x, b->temp(5), c->z, so a:=2 sets x=2, c:=a+b=7 sets z=7; (iii) by-name: substitute x for a, x*y for b, z for c — after a:=2 (x=2), reading b gives x*y=2*5=10, so c:=2+10=12 sets z=12.

steps.title: Pass-by-Value
steps.apply:
a:=2 changes local copy only; x remains 1. c:=a+b = 2+5=7 changes local copy; z remains 100.
Write prints: x=1, z=100.
steps.note: Call-by-value creates independent copies; no side effects on caller's variables.

steps.title: Pass-by-Reference
steps.formula_raw: a aliases x, b = value of x*y at call = 5 (r-value, no l-value alias), c aliases z
steps.apply:
a:=2 sets x=2. b is 5 (the expression x*y was evaluated to 5 at call time; it cannot be aliased since it's not an l-value). c:=a+b = 2+5=7, sets z=7.
Write prints: x=2, z=7.
steps.note: For call-by-reference, expressions like x*y are typically passed as their evaluated value since they have no addressable location.

steps.title: Pass-by-Name
steps.formula_raw: a is substituted by x, b is substituted by x*y (re-evaluated), c is substituted by z
steps.apply:
a:=2 is textually x:=2, so x becomes 2. Now c:=a+b is z:=x+(x*y) = 2+(2*5) = 2+10 = 12, so z=12.
Write prints: x=2, z=12.
steps.note: The key difference from by-reference: b (which is x*y) is re-evaluated AFTER x was changed to 2, giving 10 instead of 5.

solution.result: i. By-value: x=1, z=100. ii. By-reference: x=2, z=7. iii. By-name: x=2, z=12.

questions.formula_note: Call-by-name (Jensen's device): the actual argument expression is a thunk re-evaluated on each access, unlike call-by-reference where it is evaluated once at call time.
